1.synthesis: King Belshazzar makes a great feast for a thousand of his lords and drinks wine before them (v.1). While tasting the wine, he commands that the gold and silver vessels his father Nebuchadnezzar had taken from the temple in Jerusalem be brought, so that he and his lords, wives, and concubines might drink from them (vv.2–3). They drink wine and praise “the gods of gold and silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone” (v.4). The integrative thread is sacrilege as the height of arrogant impiety: Belshazzar deliberately profanes the holy vessels of God’s house in a drunken revel and toasts dead idols, flaunting contempt for the true God whose vessels he desecrates — an act of blasphemy that openly defies the God who had humbled his predecessor, and that will draw immediate and terrifying judgment.
1.historical_context: Belshazzar (v.1) was co-regent of Babylon under his father Nabonidus, “father” here meaning predecessor or ancestor, with Nebuchadnezzar as his royal forebear (v.2). Drinking from the sacred temple vessels (vv.2–3) was a calculated act of sacrilege, treating the holy things of the LORD as trophies for a drunken feast. Praising idols of gold, silver, and stone (v.4) compounded the blasphemy, defying the God whose vessels they profaned on what tradition places as the eve of Babylon’s fall.
1.literary_structure: This opening section (vv.1–4) sets the scene of sacrilege that the rest of the chapter judges, the bringing of the temple vessels (v.2) and the toast to idols (v.4) establishing the offense. The drunken feast provides the dramatic backdrop against which the mysterious handwriting will suddenly appear.
1.theological_application: To treat holy things with contempt and to exalt lifeless idols over the living God is blasphemy that invites judgment (vv.3–4; 1 Corinthians 11:27). Belshazzar’s sacrilege warns against profaning what God has set apart and against the pride that learns nothing from history — he ignored the lesson of Nebuchadnezzar’s humbling. The believer is sobered to reverence God’s holiness and to heed the warnings God has already given, lest we repeat the sins of those judged before us (1 Corinthians 10:11).
1.christology: The profaning of the temple vessels (v.2) contrasts with Christ, who cleansed the temple in zeal for his Father’s house (John 2:16–17) and who is himself the true temple (John 2:21). The idols of gold and stone that “do not see or hear” (Daniel 5:23) stand against the living God revealed in the Son, the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15). Belshazzar’s blasphemy and swift fall foreshadow the judgment awaiting all who exalt idols and despise the holy in defiance of God.

5.synthesis: Suddenly the fingers of a human hand appear and write on the plaster of the palace wall (v.5). The king’s color changes, his limbs give way, his knees knock together in terror (v.6). He summons the enchanters and astrologers, promising purple, a gold chain, and third place in the kingdom to whoever can read the writing, but none can (vv.7–9). The queen mother enters and recalls Daniel, “in whom is the spirit of the holy gods,” who interpreted dreams in Nebuchadnezzar’s day (vv.10–12). The integrative thread is sudden terror exposing the impotence of pagan wisdom: at the height of the blasphemous feast, a disembodied hand turns revelry to dread, and once again Babylon’s sages cannot read God’s message — so that, as in chapter 2, the failure of all human wisdom prepares for Daniel, the man in whom is the Spirit of God, to declare the meaning that heaven alone supplies.
5.historical_context: The mysterious hand writing on the wall (v.5) amid the feast turns the king’s arrogance to abject terror (v.6). The offer of purple, a gold chain, and “third ruler in the kingdom” (v.7) reflects Belshazzar’s position as second ruler under Nabonidus. The wise men’s failure (v.8) echoes chapter 2. The queen mother’s recollection of Daniel (vv.10–12) shows he had been sidelined under the new regime yet remembered for his proven gift.
5.literary_structure: This section (vv.5–12) builds suspense through the apparition (v.5), the king’s terror (v.6), and the failure of the sages (v.8), resolved by the queen mother’s summons of Daniel (v.12). The pattern deliberately mirrors chapter 2, setting up Daniel’s interpretation as the only true reading of God’s word.
5.theological_application: God can shatter the false security of the proud in an instant (vv.5–6). The terror that gripped Belshazzar pictures the dread that seizes the guilty conscience when confronted with God’s judgment (Hebrews 10:31). The repeated impotence of worldly wisdom before God’s word (v.8) teaches that the things of God are spiritually discerned (1 Corinthians 2:14), and that God reserves the understanding of his message for those to whom he gives his Spirit.
5.christology: The hand that writes judgment on the wall (v.5) recalls that God writes both condemnation and grace — the law written by the finger of God (Exodus 31:18), and the record of debt that Christ cancels, nailing it to the cross (Colossians 2:14). Where pagan wisdom cannot read God’s word, Christ is the one who opens the scroll and its seals (Revelation 5:5). The terror of the guilty before the writing points to the need for the Mediator who turns the verdict against us into pardon for all who are his.

13.synthesis: Daniel is brought before the king, who asks whether he is the exile from Judah whom his father brought from Jerusalem (v.13). Belshazzar recounts hearing that “the spirit of the gods” is in Daniel, with light, understanding, and excellent wisdom (v.14). He admits that the wise men could not read the writing (v.15), and offers Daniel purple, a gold chain, and third rank if he can interpret it (v.16). The integrative thread is the desperate appeal of a doomed king to the prophet he had ignored: only in crisis does Belshazzar summon Daniel, dangling rewards for the interpretation — yet the very offer reveals his shallow concern for prestige rather than repentance, setting up Daniel’s refusal of the gifts and his unflinching declaration of judgment, for the man of God cannot be bought and will speak truth regardless of reward.
13.historical_context: That Belshazzar must ask whether Daniel is the famous exile (v.13) shows how far Daniel had been forgotten under the later regime, though now advanced in years. The king’s recital of Daniel’s reputation (v.14) and renewed offer of rewards (v.16) reflect his desperation. The scene echoes Pharaoh summoning Joseph (Genesis 41:14–15), the forgotten man of God called in crisis to interpret what none else can. The king’s ignorance of so renowned a figure dramatizes the moral decline of the regime: the wisdom that had served Nebuchadnezzar so visibly (Daniel 2:48) had been pushed to the margins by a generation that neither knew nor sought it, until catastrophe forced its recovery.
13.literary_structure: This brief section (vv.13–16) brings Daniel onstage, the king’s questions and recital of his reputation (vv.13–14) leading to the renewed offer of reward (v.16). It sets up Daniel’s pointed refusal of the gifts and his bold rehearsal of Belshazzar’s guilt that follows. The escalating offer of reward — purple, gold chain, and third rank (v.16) — repeats the terms already promised to the failed sages (Daniel 5:7), so that Daniel’s coming refusal of them all sets his integrity in sharp relief against a court that thought everything had its price.
13.theological_application: People often turn to God’s servants only in crisis, seeking relief rather than repentance (vv.13–16). The believer is reminded that the world may ignore the things of God until disaster strikes, and that faithful witness is needed precisely then. Daniel’s coming readiness to speak regardless of reward models the integrity of one who serves God rather than human favor (Galatians 1:10), unmoved by either threat or bribe.
13.christology: Daniel, the long-overlooked man of God summoned in the hour of crisis (v.13), foreshadows Christ, the stone rejected by the builders who becomes the only hope in the day of reckoning (Acts 4:11). As Daniel would not be bought to soften God’s word (Daniel 5:17), so Christ spoke the truth without regard to the favor of men (Matthew 22:16). The world’s belated, self-interested appeal to the prophet points to the urgency of seeking God before the night of judgment falls.

17.synthesis: Daniel refuses the gifts but agrees to read the writing (v.17). He reminds Belshazzar how God gave Nebuchadnezzar greatness, and how, when his heart was lifted up in pride, he was humbled until he knew “that the Most High God rules the kingdom of mankind” (vv.18–21). “And you his son, Belshazzar, have not humbled your heart, though you knew all this,” but lifted yourself against the Lord of heaven, profaning his vessels and praising idols, not honoring “the God in whose hand is your breath” (vv.22–23). The writing — MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN — means God has numbered the kingdom and ended it, weighed the king and found him wanting, and divided his kingdom to the Medes and Persians (vv.24–28). The integrative thread is judgment pronounced on willful pride that refused the lesson of history: Belshazzar’s sin is graver than Nebuchadnezzar’s because he “knew all this” yet did not humble himself — and the verdict, numbered, weighed, and divided, declares that the God who holds his very breath has measured him and found him fatally wanting.
17.historical_context: Daniel’s refusal of reward (v.17) underscores his incorruptible integrity. His historical review (vv.18–21) recalls chapter 4, indicting Belshazzar for ignoring its lesson though he “knew all this” (v.22). The Aramaic words (v.25) are units of weight (mina, shekel, half-shekels) reinterpreted as verbs: numbered, weighed, divided (vv.26–28). “Found wanting” (v.27) employs the image of weighing in the balance (Job 31:6), and the kingdom’s division to the Medes and Persians (v.28) was fulfilled that very night.
17.literary_structure: This central section (vv.17–28) joins prophetic rebuke (vv.22–23) to the interpretation of the writing (vv.25–28). The contrast with Nebuchadnezzar (vv.18–21) sharpens Belshazzar’s guilt, and the threefold verdict — numbered, weighed, divided — forms the chapter’s climactic sentence of judgment.
17.theological_application: Sin against the light of known truth is more culpable: Belshazzar “knew all this” yet did not humble his heart (v.22; Luke 12:47). The image of being “weighed and found wanting” (v.27) warns that every life is measured against God’s standard, and that the God “in whose hand is your breath” (v.23) holds our times. The believer is called to humble the heart before this God, learning from the warnings of the past rather than presuming, and finding in Christ the only righteousness that is not found wanting.
17.christology: To be “weighed… and found wanting” (v.27) is the verdict every sinner deserves before the holy God in whose hand is our breath (v.23). Only Christ, weighed in the balance of God’s justice, was not found wanting, the one in whom the Father was well pleased (Matthew 3:17). He bore the judgment of the numbered and divided kingdom of sin, that those found wanting in themselves might be clothed in his righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21) and stand in the day when God numbers and weighs all things.

29.synthesis: Belshazzar commands that Daniel be clothed in purple, given a gold chain, and proclaimed third ruler in the kingdom (v.29). “That very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed” (v.30), “and Darius the Mede received the kingdom, being about sixty-two years old” (v.31). The integrative thread is the swift, exact fulfillment of God’s sentence: the words were scarcely interpreted before the kingdom was numbered and ended that very night, the empire passing to the Medes and Persians just as Daniel declared — a stark demonstration that when God has weighed a kingdom and found it wanting, no honors, defenses, or delay can avert the judgment he has decreed.
29.historical_context: Belshazzar’s honoring of Daniel (v.29) even as judgment loomed shows the futility of the gesture. His death “that very night” (v.30) corresponds to the fall of Babylon to the Medo-Persian forces in 539 BC. “Darius the Mede” (v.31) — variously identified, possibly a throne name or a governor under Cyrus — receives the kingdom, fulfilling the “divided… to the Medes and Persians” of the writing (Daniel 5:28).
29.literary_structure: This brief closing section (vv.29–31) records the immediate fulfillment, the honoring of Daniel (v.29) ironically followed at once by the king’s death (v.30) and the transfer of power (v.31). The speed of the fulfillment seals the chapter’s message and bridges to the new Medo-Persian setting of chapter 6.
29.theological_application: When God pronounces judgment, no honor or defense can avert it; Babylon fell “that very night” (v.30). The believer is reminded that the proud security of the wicked is fragile, and that God’s word, once spoken, is sure (Numbers 23:19). The sudden end of a kingdom that seemed invincible warns against trusting earthly power and calls us to be found ready, for judgment can come swiftly and without further warning (Luke 12:20). The honoring of Daniel even as the kingdom collapsed (v.29) shows how empty earthly reward is when divorced from repentance; the gold chain adorned a man whose king had hours to live. The believer is reminded that the favor of the powerful is fleeting and that true and lasting honor comes from God alone (John 12:26).
29.christology: The sudden fall of Babylon in a single night (v.30) prefigures the fall of “Babylon the great” in Revelation, judged in one hour (Revelation 18:10), and warns of the day when the Lord comes as a thief upon a heedless world (1 Thessalonians 5:2–3). Against the kingdom numbered and ended stands the kingdom of Christ that shall never end (Daniel 7:14). The believer’s security is not in empires that fall in a night but in the King whose dominion is everlasting. That the kingdom passed in a single night to another (vv.30–31) reminds us that all dominion is held at God’s pleasure and given finally to the Son of Man, whose reception of the kingdom, unlike Belshazzar’s loss of it, can never be reversed (Daniel 7:14; Hebrews 12:28).
